brief_notes: Compelling computational neuroscience paper demonstrating that competitive (inhibitory) inter-regional interactions are essential for whole-brain modeling across three species. The cross-species design (human, macaque, mouse) strengthens the evolutionary conservation claim, and the reservoir computing analysis linking competitive interactions to computational capacity is novel. Tables are well-structured with clear model comparisons. Weaknesses include relatively small non-human samples (19 macaque, 10 mouse), the cooperative-only baseline for macaque/mouse is missing from the information-theoretic table, and the Hopf bifurcation model simplification limits biological specificity. Statistical details (effect sizes, confidence intervals) are sparse.
